sections.title: Reflexive Pronouns
sections.explanation: Reflexive verbs describe actions a subject performs on themselves. They are identified by the pronoun 'se' attached to the infinitive (e.g., lavarse — to wash oneself). When conjugating, the 'se' becomes a reflexive pronoun that matches the subject: me (yo), te (tú), se (él/ella/usted), nos (nosotros), os (vosotros), se (ellos/ellas/ustedes). The pronoun is placed directly before the conjugated verb: Yo me lavo. Tú te lavas. Él se lava. Nosotros nos lavamos. Vosotros os laváis. Ellos se lavan. With infinitives and gerunds, the pronoun may also be attached to the end: Voy a lavarme. / Estoy lavándome.
sections.tip: The reflexive pronoun must always agree with the subject. A quick memory trick: think of the pronoun as a mirror — it reflects the action back to the person doing it. Me, te, se, nos, os, se.

examples.es: Ella se mira en el espejo.
examples.en: She looks at herself in the mirror.
We get ourselves ready to go out.
You all enjoy yourselves a lot.
They know each other very well.

exercises.prompt: Which reflexive pronoun goes with 'yo'?
exercises.explanation: The reflexive pronoun for yo is 'me'. Yo me lavo = I wash myself.

exercises.prompt: Tú ___ lavas las manos. (You wash your hands.)
exercises.hint: The reflexive pronoun for tú is one letter: t_
exercises.explanation: The reflexive pronoun for tú is 'te'. Tú te lavas las manos.

exercises.prompt: Which reflexive pronoun goes with 'nosotros'?
exercises.explanation: The reflexive pronoun for nosotros is 'nos'. Nosotros nos lavamos = We wash ourselves.

exercises.prompt: Ellos ___ preparan para la fiesta. (They get ready for the party.)
exercises.hint: The reflexive pronoun for ellos/ellas is the same as for él/ella
exercises.explanation: Both él/ella and ellos/ellas use 'se' as their reflexive pronoun.

exercises.prompt: Where does the reflexive pronoun go with a conjugated verb?
options: At the end of the sentence
exercises.explanation: With a conjugated verb the reflexive pronoun comes directly before it: Yo me lavo (not Yo lavo me).

exercises.prompt: Which sentence is correct?
exercises.explanation: The reflexive pronoun 'se' goes directly before the conjugated verb: Ella se peina.

exercises.prompt: Vosotros ___ divertís en la playa. (You all enjoy yourselves at the beach.)
exercises.hint: The reflexive pronoun for vosotros is 'os'
exercises.explanation: The reflexive pronoun for vosotros is 'os'. Vosotros os divertís en la playa.

exercises.prompt: How can the reflexive pronoun be placed with an infinitive?
options: Attached to the end of the infinitive
Reflexive pronouns cannot be used with infinitives
exercises.explanation: With infinitives the pronoun can be attached to the end (lavarse, peinarse) or placed before the auxiliary verb: Me voy a lavar / Voy a lavarme. Both are correct.

sections.title: Daily Routine Verbs
sections.explanation: Many daily routine actions in Spanish are expressed with reflexive verbs. The most common ones are: despertarse (to wake up), levantarse (to get up), ducharse (to shower), lavarse (to wash oneself), peinarse (to comb one's hair), vestirse (to get dressed), and acostarse (to go to bed / to lie down). Note that several of these are stem-changing: despertarse (e→ie): me despierto, te despiertas, se despierta, nos despertamos, os despertáis, se despiertan. Vestirse (e→i): me visto, te vistes, se viste, nos vestimos, os vestís, se visten. Acostarse (o→ue): me acuesto, te acuestas, se acuesta, nos acostamos, os acostáis, se acuestan. Ducharse and levantarse are regular.
sections.tip: Remember that stem changes do NOT occur in the nosotros and vosotros forms — these are called the 'boot verbs' because if you draw a box around the forms that change, it looks like a boot (all except nosotros and vosotros).

examples.es: Me despierto a las siete de la mañana.
examples.en: I wake up at seven in the morning.

examples.es: Él se levanta tarde los fines de semana.
examples.en: He gets up late on weekends.

examples.es: Nosotros nos duchamos después del gimnasio.
Los niños se acuestan a las nueve.
examples.en: The children go to bed at nine.

examples.es: Ella se peina frente al espejo.
examples.en: She combs her hair in front of the mirror.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'I wake up at 7 a.m.' in Spanish?
exercises.explanation: Despertarse (e→ie) conjugates as me despierto for yo. The stem changes e→ie in all forms except nosotros/vosotros.

exercises.prompt: Ella se ___ (duchar) todos los días. (She showers every day.)
exercises.hint: Ducharse is a regular -AR reflexive verb; she = ella → third person singular
exercises.explanation: Ducharse is regular: duc h + a = ducha. Ella se ducha todos los días.

exercises.prompt: Which is the correct nosotros form of 'acostarse'?
exercises.explanation: Stem-changing verbs do NOT change in nosotros. Acostarse (o→ue) → nosotros nos acostamos (no stem change).

exercises.prompt: Yo me ___ (vestir) antes del desayuno. (I get dressed before breakfast.)
exercises.hint: Vestirse is an e→i stem-changing verb; yo form has stem change
exercises.explanation: Vestirse (e→i): yo me visto. The stem changes from vest- to vist- in the yo form.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'The children go to bed at 9' in Spanish?
options: Los niños se acuestan a las 9.
Los niños se acostean a las 9.
Los niños se acostan a las 9.
exercises.explanation: Acostarse (o→ue): ellos se acuestan. The o→ue stem change applies to ellos. 'Acostean' and 'acostan' are not valid forms.

exercises.prompt: Nosotros nos ___ (levantar) a las seis. (We get up at six.)
exercises.hint: Levantarse is regular; nosotros ending for -AR verbs is -amos
exercises.explanation: Levantarse is regular: levant + amos = levantamos. Nosotros nos levantamos a las seis.

exercises.prompt: Put the daily routine in the most logical order:
exercises.explanation: A typical daily sequence: wake up (despertarse) → get up (levantarse) → shower (ducharse) → get dressed (vestirse) → go to bed (acostarse).

exercises.prompt: Tú te ___ (despertar) cuando suena el despertador. (You wake up when the alarm goes off.)
exercises.hint: Despertarse is e→ie; tú ending is -as, but the stem changes
exercises.explanation: Despertarse (e→ie): tú te despiertas. The e in the stem changes to ie: despert- → despiert-.

sections.title: Verbs That Change Meaning
sections.explanation: Some Spanish verbs have a completely different meaning when used reflexively. Understanding these pairs is essential for avoiding confusion. Key pairs: ir (to go) → irse (to leave, to go away): Voy al mercado. (I'm going to the market.) vs. Me voy. (I'm leaving.) dormir (to sleep) → dormirse (to fall asleep): El bebé duerme. (The baby sleeps.) vs. El bebé se duerme. (The baby falls asleep.) llamar (to call) → llamarse (to be named): Te llamo mañana. (I'll call you tomorrow.) vs. Me llamo Ana. (My name is Ana / I am called Ana.) Other important pairs: poner (to put) → ponerse (to put on clothing / to become): Pongo el libro. vs. Me pongo el abrigo. / Me pongo nervioso. quitar (to remove) → quitarse (to take off clothing): Quita el plato. vs. Me quito los zapatos.
sections.tip: When you see llamarse, the literal meaning is 'to call oneself'. So '¿Cómo te llamas?' literally means 'What do you call yourself?' — a great way to remember that llamarse = to be named.

examples.en: What is your name? (lit. What do you call yourself?)

examples.es: El niño se duerme en el sofá.
examples.en: The child falls asleep on the sofa.

examples.es: Me pongo el abrigo porque hace frío.
examples.en: I put on my coat because it's cold.

examples.es: Me quito los zapatos al entrar.
examples.en: I take off my shoes when entering.

exercises.prompt: What does 'Me llamo Pedro' mean?
exercises.explanation: Llamarse means 'to be named / to call oneself'. Me llamo Pedro = My name is Pedro (literally: I call myself Pedro).

exercises.prompt: What is the difference between 'El bebé duerme' and 'El bebé se duerme'?
options: 'duerme' = the baby sleeps; 'se duerme' = the baby falls asleep
'duerme' = the baby falls asleep; 'se duerme' = the baby sleeps
exercises.explanation: Dormir = to sleep (a state). Dormirse = to fall asleep (a change of state / process). El bebé duerme = The baby is sleeping. El bebé se duerme = The baby is falling asleep.

exercises.prompt: Ella ___ ___ temprano porque tiene trabajo. (She leaves early because she has work.) [Use irse]
exercises.hint: Irse in the ella form: the reflexive pronoun is 'se' and ir conjugates to 'va'
exercises.explanation: Irse (to leave/go away): ella se va. Ir conjugates irregularly: voy, vas, va, vamos, vais, van.

exercises.prompt: Which sentence means 'I put on my jacket'?
exercises.explanation: Ponerse = to put on (clothing). Me pongo la chaqueta = I put on my jacket. 'Pongo la chaqueta' means I put the jacket (somewhere), not on myself.

exercises.prompt: ¿Cómo ___ ___ (llamarse) tu profesora? (What is your teacher's name?)
exercises.hint: Llamarse for ella/él/usted: reflexive pronoun 'se' + llama
exercises.explanation: Llamarse (to be named): ella se llama. ¿Cómo se llama tu profesora? = What is your teacher's name?

exercises.prompt: What does 'Voy al banco' mean, and how is it different from 'Me voy'?
options: 'Voy al banco' = I'm going to the bank; 'Me voy' = I'm leaving
'Voy al banco' = I'm leaving the bank; 'Me voy' = I'm going somewhere
'Me voy' is not a real expression
exercises.explanation: Ir = to go (toward a destination). Irse = to leave, to go away. Voy al banco specifies a destination; Me voy just means I'm heading out / leaving.

exercises.prompt: Los estudiantes ___ ___ (dormirse) en clase. (The students fall asleep in class.)
exercises.hint: Dormirse (o→ue): ellos form → reflexive pronoun 'se' + duermen
exercises.explanation: Dormirse (o→ue): ellos se duermen. The stem changes o→ue in all forms except nosotros/vosotros.

exercises.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'quitarse'?
options: Me quito el sombrero al entrar.
exercises.explanation: Quitarse = to take off (clothing). Me quito el sombrero = I take off my hat. The reflexive pronoun 'me' must agree with the subject yo, and goes before the conjugated verb.
